title: Psalms 74
subtitle: Why Have You Rejected Us Forever?

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “They Burned Your Sanctuary to the Ground”

heb.paragraph: "They burned your sanctuary (miqdashĕk̅ā) to the ground; they defiled the dwelling place of your Name" (v.7). A communal lament over the temple’s destruction. The enemy smashed carved panels (v.6), set the meeting place on fire (v.8), destroyed all the sanctuaries in the land (v.8). "We are given no signs from God; no prophets are left" (v.9). The silence of God: no miracles, no prophetic word, no indication of how long.

notes.note: "Why have you rejected us forever, O God?" The "forever" is the anguish. Temporary rejection is discipline; permanent rejection is abandonment.

"They burned your sanctuary. They destroyed every place where God was worshipped. We are given no signs; no prophets are left." Triple desolation: temple destroyed, sanctuaries burned, prophetic silence. God’s presence seems erased from the earth.

refs.note: Lamentations describes the same temple destruction with God as the destroyer of his own sanctuary.
The Babylonian destruction of temple, walls, and treasures fulfills the psalm's lament.
Jesus's prediction that not one temple stone will remain echoes the destruction imagery.

hist.context: Psalm 74 is a communal lament over the temple's destruction, likely the Babylonian devastation of 586 BC. The description of axes and hatchets smashing carved paneling (vv.5-6) provides vivid detail. The complaint 'we are given no signs; there is no longer any prophet' (v.9) reflects the silence of the exile period when prophetic voices seemed absent.

sections.header: Verses 12–23 — “But God Is My King from Long Ago”

heb.paragraph: "But God is my King (melek̅) from long ago; he brings salvation on the earth" (v.12). The pivot: from destruction to creation memory. God split the sea (v.13), crushed Leviathan (v.14), opened springs and dried up rivers (v.15), established sun, moon, seasons (vv.16–17). The argument: the God who created the world can restore the temple. "Rise up, O God, and defend your cause" (v.22).

notes.note: "But God is my King from long ago." The creation-recital as argument for restoration. The same God who split the sea and crushed Leviathan can rebuild what enemies destroyed.

"Remember how the enemy has mocked you. Do not hand over the life of your dove to wild beasts; do not forget the lives of your afflicted people forever." The plea: remember, do not forget, do not hand over. Three negatives to counter the silence.
"Rise up, O God, and defend YOUR cause." The argument shifts: this is not just our problem — it is yours. They mocked YOUR name. Defend YOUR honour.

refs.note: The appeal to the arm that cut Rahab in pieces uses the same creation-combat mythology.
God crushing Rahab and piercing the gliding serpent parallels the psalm's Leviathan reference.
The great dragon defeated by Michael develops the cosmic conflict imagery.

hist.context: The appeal to creation (vv.12-17) recalls God's primordial victory over chaos — crushing Leviathan's heads, establishing day and night, setting earth's boundaries. This cosmic power is invoked to address historical crisis: the God who conquered chaos can surely defeat Babylon. The psalm moves from lament through creation confession to urgent petition.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "Why have you rejected us forever, O God?" The "forever" is the anguish…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVRemember the nation you purchased long ago, the people of your inheritance, whom you redeemed— Mount Zion, where you dwelt.ESVRemember your congregation, which you have purchased of old, which you have redeemed to be the tribe of your heritage! Remember Mount Zion, where you have dwelt.NIVThey burned your sanctuary to the ground; they defiled the dwelling place of your Name.ESVThey set your sanctuary on fire; they profaned the dwelling place of your name, bringing it down to the ground.

rec.text: ""Why have you rejected us forever, O God?" The "forever" is the anguish. Temporary rejection is discipline; permanent rejection is abandonment."

rows.label: The Devastation — 74:1–11
rows.text: "They burned your sanctuary." No signs, no prophets. "How long?"

hebtext.note: "They burned your sanctuary (miqdashĕk̅ā) to the ground; they defiled the dwelling place of your Name" (v.7). A communal lament over the temple’s destruction. The enemy smashed carved panels (v.6), set the meeting place on fire (v.8), destroyed all the sanctuaries in the land (v.8). "We are given no signs from God; no prophets are left" (v.9). The silence of God: no miracles, no prophetic word, no indication of how long.

"But God is my King (melek̅) from long ago; he brings salvation on the earth" (v.12). The pivot: from destruction to creation memory. God split the sea (v.13), crushed Leviathan (v.14), opened springs and dried up rivers (v.15), established sun, moon, seasons (vv.16–17). The argument: the God who created the world can restore the temple. "Rise up, O God, and defend your cause" (v.22).

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: creation power as argument for restoration. Judgment: temple destruction as divine judgment. Covenant: "do not forget your covenant" (v.20).
